commentBody: That went quickly ... until the upper middle area remained. Then it went very slowly. I haven't quite understood the relation of the clues to the answers, but plan to remedy that problem posthaste.
I deem this a good Wednesday.

Even as a Jew, I think the [Matthew, Luke, John] angle would have been better.

Definitely a Twednesday in my book.

I found this late in the day today (Tuesday), and though I put it into the Tuesday comments (where it really belongs), I know it was too late for many to have seen it. It relates to a nouveau slang word that was in the Tuesday puzzle, which some of the older folks got a little cuckoo over. Here it is: This article just came over my Apple News feed: https://apple.news/AwyVN7qdHQDm6PUKPbWQoew And it’s about something from the 80s!
Welcome back, Wayne, solo this time, and thanks for giving us a Wednesday-like Wednesday.
This gen Xer had never heard CUT A RUG. I had heard all the others, though.

I personally find that Wednesdays have the most variable difficulty.  Sometimes, they are very straightforward, and sometimes, they really, really aren't.

Saturdays used to have more variability as well, but they've recently seemed much easier than they used to be.

Clever theme.  I was looking for other people named Mark and other string instruments until the actual way to answer the theme questions clicked.

A lot of good fill, not too many proper nouns that depend on specific spheres of knowledge.  EDSEL/TEL was the last square I got cycling all letters.  Other clues that I found tough like EYELETS and DRIBLET had easier crossings.

Very clever and fun.  Enjoyed the theme a lot.  The cluing made me wonder where the author was going and I felt as though it wasn’t a give away. 

Some of the clues did a very good job of polysimny and made me suss out the area more often than not. 

On a slightly different note, I’m finally at December 2014 in the archives.  Met a Tuesday that’s as tough as a modern Saturday.  Nearly wept.

[Basket, ____,  Dodge, Foot]

DROP THE BALL

Quick solve, fun theme.

Brilliant and fun – 
I wish it had been Sunday-sized!

Thank you so much, Mr. Bergman, and congrats on a worthy first solo!

Ohhh, Pay for Play stuck me for a while. Diabolical.

Nice puzzle!

Using a word I learned today in a sentence:

I am going to have a driblet or two or three of scotch.

I liked the puzzle a lot.  Good thing I finally added the TIGER to EASY, and I was sure of SKIP A BEAT, because I got pretty stuck with DRopLET.

About like my normal Wednesday time, I think.  So it seemed to me like it was in the Wednesday zone, for the most part.

So maybe I can come out of hiding to look at the comments today

[Bossa, _____, swing, rock]

MISS A BEAT


[Sharp, _____, three-point, U]

LOSE A TURN

[Ace, _____, 3, 4]

DROP A DEUCE

commentBody:
Regarding the Mini 1A, a question for Star Wars geeks:

Is "Any mentee of Yoda" really a JEDI, though?

Aren't only some mentees of Yoda jedis?

After all, if you haven't finished your training aren't you still just a padawan (no shade on padawans)?

"Any graduate student of Yoda" would have sat better for me.

SALT AWAY
I would call this an Enthuseday puzzle, a little harder than a usual Wednesday, but a lot of fun to work.

I honestly haven’t done a themed early week puzzle in private a year. 
I thought today was Thursday.  But obviously Wednesday. 

Glad I was mistaken. This was ‘hard’. Okay, not hard. But when you have DRopLET locked in. 4 squares are absolutely confounding. 

Fun theme.

"I can't find my guitar!"
"I wouldn't fret about it."

("Just my pluck.")

I thought this was a terrific Wednesday. It is the first puzzle I put in my early-week  POY list. A lot to like:
—Fun, creative theme. Even though I got the idea after MISSTHEMARK, the others weren’t gimmes; I got TAKETHECAKE immediately, but had SKIPAPAGE initially, and tried to shoehorn in NOSTRINGSATTACHED which was a little off the theme (and didn’t fit anyway) but I thought it could have worked since the two strings were separated
—Elegant construction. Great use of reflective symmetry to make this work for 5 good themers (two interlocking) and plenty of room for some other long entries especially the top middle. Not a lot of glue 
—some clever clues and misdirection: Head covering, Pay for play, mini mart, test the waters
—just about perfect difficulty level for a Wednesday, in my opinion. Enough to chew on but enough, I hope, to give early week solvers a foothold.

BTW  anyone else have TIKIBAR before SAKEBAR?

Well done Wayne!
This was quite pleasant to solve, but it took Tuesday time...
12D almost got me!! The pesky 'K'! Had to fight through it because I was determined to solve a puzzle with TAKIS in it!
Oops, ___ , Shute, Drat, Uh oh. https://www.youtube.com/shorts/YnpFbQ3Qeww

To all of you offering hilarious alternate possibilities for this ingenious theme, thank you.

What a great, creative, funny place this is!

25A CUT A RUG

Today I learned.

I appreciate the shoutout Mr. Bergman, and I can confirm - CUT A RUG crossing a 60s song and a 50s movie was definitely painful for my Gen Z brain! This hobby sure is hard when you’re not the intended audience. Ah, at least we have Lara Raj in yesterday’s midi.
Really fun puzzle today. The theme was great.
It certainly misses the mark when the pejorative 'scab' is used.  Disappointing.
Can anyone explain what skip a beat has to do with newspaper sections?
In Wednesday Midi: polka is not traditional Bavarian music. Perhaps Bohemia?

This was a fun puzzle. Was it just me, or was the intended meaning/wordplay of "Mini mart?" (CAR LOT) as it's explained in this article a bit of a stretch? It makes sense if you know the answer, but even setting the dastardly title case/proper noun switcheroo aside, it's such a specific meaning for such an ambiguous word ("Mini"), and then you still have to figure out the pun itself.

Most wordplay clues have an "aha" moment even if you end up finding the answer indirectly, but I got it from the crosses and was still confused. Maybe it’s just a personal stump though.

Very creative theme with some excellent cluing. 

[Pay for play, perhaps] for TYPO and [Mini mart?] for CAR LOT.  Just perfect.
LOVED this debut puzzle!  Keep it up!
Best theme in a while. Laughed out loud multiple times, very clever

Is there a bug?  I finished both MiDi puzzles today, Wednesday, and Saturday, on those days.  They show completed, all blue squares, but no star.   What am I missing?  All the other days are finished and starred.

[Lettuce, ____, tomato, bacon]


HOLD THE MAYO!

Nice one.

Definitely a Whursday solve for me; Thank You, Mr. Bergman! Whilst not metallic herself, Venus has a particular affinity to the element copper, as any good alchemist knows. After all, much ancient copper was mined on her home island of Cyprus ("copper"<--"cuprum"<--"Kypros"). Although there is a competing theory that Bronze Age Minoans sailed across the Mediterranean, through the Pillars of Hercules, traversed the Atlantic, sailed up the St. Lawrence (by which point traversing the Niagara Escarpment was child's play), entered the Great Lakes, sailed up the Detroit River (stopping for lunch at the Edsel Ford House and Museum in Grosse Pointe Shores), and into the Upper Lakes, to the copper mines by Lake Superior. Which may pretty whackadoodle to you, but hey! There are those Linear B tablets I found on the Keweenaw Peninsula while on vacation there last summer! Here's Ottorino Respighi's take on Botticelli's famous painting "The Slurpable Venus": https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=08bm32R5UfM&list=RD08bm32R5UfM&start_radio=1 *** I know it's a mainly vehicle for Cynthia, and not Ariana, but here's a bassless, viola-less cover of "Defying Gravity": https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1VmavtvABxk&list=RD1VmavtvABxk&start_radio=1 (Based on their video library, I would hire the Chapel Hill Duo for my next to-do:-)

I finally figured out my problem with the Midi - the timer.  I turned that off and find I enjoy the puzzle a lot more.  Why?  

I do the Mini for speed and saw that I was rushing through the Midi.  So like the big puzzle I'll take my time and enjoy it more.

To the crossword puzzle creators:

First a huge thank you for sharing your creativity and making puzzles for us to solve. Regardless of ease or difficulty, I enjoy the solving process. I can either know, deduce, or research the answer.

Second, never, ever apologize for having a clue and answer that another generation may not know. Period! Please note this Wayne Bergman.

Happy midweek solving everyone.

Just as I was reminding myself not to get all riled up by a puzzle I DEEM "too easy," along comes this jolly piece of work with its creative clues and fresh TAKE.  I could supply missing words, but that wasn't necessarily a help; (I don't know any type of carpet/RUG that starts with CU-...)  
In short order, then, I caught on and romped through the rest of the puzzle with pleasure.

I just had my six-monthly dental appt....very little TARTAR.  (If you floss after every meal because your teeth are very crowded, it's quite beneficial.)  Nevertheless, I was slow to come up with 1D, trying to think of versions of 'gingivitis' and briefly trying CARIES.

Have never heard of TAKIS (regional?)-- and entered OH I SEE before revising it to OK.

Who says a PEST is a "minor menace"?  Fire ants are quite small, as are black widow spiders, but they're more than mere PESTs, I say.  On the other hand, maybe Wayne was thinking of "minor" as "a 15-year-old," so it's hard not to agree.  (Ducking and running...)

This was a fun puzzle to solve -- and I imagine it was a fun puzzle to brainstorm too.  All those items that go missing by means of a different verb.  Finding the apt phrases, making sure they're symmetrical, putting them in both Across and Down answers -- very, very neatly done. And no junky fill.  I applaud your idea and I applaud your execution, Wayne.

But you could have had one more theme answer in the grid without changing anything other than one clue.  For 8D, you could have had "Washington Post, ______, Boston Globe."  PAPERLESS!  No, you don't need to have NEWSPAPERLESS, either.  "Was your PAPER delivered today?"  "No, I didn't get my PAPER today."

I'm thinking, Wayne, that PAPERLESS was part of your theme set when you submitted the puzzle and that the editors said: "Nope, you can't use that because it's not verb followed by object of the verb like the other themers.  The theme answers need to be cohesive.  But no need to redo the grid -- just clue PAPERLESS as a non-theme answer.  Am I right?  (Haven't read your Constructor's Note yet.)

Anyway, I enjoyed solving this light and breezy puzzle.
Very fun

Friday is FRIED-day
Saturday is AHHH-day (because it is the most relaxing day of the week for me.

Kenny — I, too, had droplet first. (But DRIBLET is an entirely valid word.)

@Steve
What about 'dribs and drabs'?
More generally, -let is a productive suffix in English. You can add it to any noun to convey the quality of smallness. 
State-let? Delaware.
Star-let? Kristin Chenoweth. (Although 'starlet' has morphed into its own word.)
Lake-let? What the melting snow is forming at the end of your driveway.

See also -ette, -ling, and other diminutives.

The article was intended for people who listened to music in the 80s, and would also not be inclined to say that it slaps (but still might understand it).

@Bob
Boomer here.  I remember CUTARUG from a print version of the Sunday NYT probably 30+ yrs ago in which I had goofed a couple of crosses and ended up with RUTARUT.  My MIL found this quite amusing!

@Bob

The phrase CUT A RUG gives me the mental image of flappers doing the jitterbug in black and white... This Gen-Xer was really into all things 1920's for a while in my teens — in no small part because of the Ragtime film that was released in 1981 (even though it was filmed in color!).

@G

Ugh, that was a very awkward run-on sentence! The "color" reference was related to my seeing the jitterbug in black and white in my mind's eye, even though the film that spurred my interest was filmed in color...

@G
Stop me before I clause again!!!
@Chris thank you so much! I didn’t know EDSEL either. I had sEEM, which doesn’t exactly work now that I reread it.
@Chris Edsel shows up a lot, actually.  Ford will never live it down

@Chris
I went a couple of directions on the theme while working the across clues. I put viola at the start of 55A. That didn't work well. Super misdirects. My favorite was TOILETLID for 32D: Head covering. Funny!

@Red Carpet Your comment had me curious, so to the December 2014 archive I immediately went to try and find, and solve, what you were referencing.  Might you have been referring to the Tuesday, December 2 puzzle?  If so, I agree that the difficulty was of a “late week” puzzle by current standards, though perhaps not quite a Saturday.  If you weren’t referring to December 2, and there is a harder Tuesday puzzle to be had in that calendar month then, well, wowza!

@Anonymous
Your laughter might be wry or even rueful.

@Anita

Nice try, but the blank spot doesn't fit.

@B

None of the theme answers are members of the list, but rather categories describing the others.  You have to imagine some other ball, Mark, rug, etc., that is missing.

@Elbridge Gerry

Yeah, I think the problem is that two of three of Anita's examples contain the word ball in the name itself, e.g., basketball and football. (Dodge ball is two words, at least...)

I love the concept though! 

Perhaps:
[Nerf, ____, rubber, squash]

@Anita
I'm with you on this. I think this works fine and is a nice addition to the themers. Ignore those nit-niks nesting below!

@G

To match the puzzle, the items in the list must be examples of the answer. A basket is not a ball so it's a no go. But I did say "nice try" and liked the effort.

@sotto voce
Great puzzle, but not a debut. His first puzzle was 3/19/2020.
@Times Rita sitting voce noted first “solo” which is accurate. His debut was co-constructed with Gary.

@Times Rita

Well, then he should get busy; some of us can't wait six years between puzzles!

@Francis sure, let’s go with that.

@Ace @Long

you guys have won “thread of the year” in my book.

commentBody: @Mike The police are looking into recent guitar thefts and say they’ve got no leads.
@Mike Actually i noticed that you might have lost your rhythm (i missed you yesterday!)
@Petrol  That's right, so they're going to amp up their efforts.

@Mike
I imagine you're as cabo-ble at playing the guitar as you are punning.
@Mike if you’ve lost your usual acoustic guitar, may i suggest….Steal strings?

@Mike

Some strumpet probably swiped it.

@jma
Shouldn't that be the CAPO di tutti capi? Unless, of course, your last name is San Lucas.

@SP

It lightens my mood, a bit, to have enjoyed a puzzle that you also enjoyed.

I fear I was born with a broken sense of ...  I don't know how to express it...without a firm sense of how to evaluate things, how to rank things.

As a result I like almost everything about as much as I do anything else.  The most praised puzzle I've seen here was certainly delightful, but only modestly more than the standard, to me.

I think I'm missing the highs and lows, as the muse said in "Desperado".

If you've read "Zen and the Art of Motor Cycle Maintenance",  you might say that I'm "Quality Blind".  

And I'm not even sure if that's a bad thing.

@SP
USAA saved me from that...

A big part of my life...

36D's clue was so sly!  I wonder how many clues were original....

@Mean Old Lady
I thought it was great to see USAA make a debut. We've been with them for over 30 years. A big part of our life, too.

@dutchiris
SHUTE?
Shoot?

Remembering a time when it was controversial to have DAMN in a movie.....

@Mean Old Lady
Google says SHUTE is the way to go.

@Andrzej

😂😂....hey, wait I don't get it...do I just continue to fake it in the hopes that Andrzej isn't laying a trap for me, or do I admit that I don't get it and risk losing street cred on the mean streets of this forum.

"How could he not know what 'rocket' is an call himself a human.", they'll be saying.

I think I better be sure I click "Cancel" on this post.

@Andrzej

Ok, now I think I get it.  

Yeah, rocket strike is right.

Unless that's not what you meant, then, just ignore this solemn agreement.

Rocket is what Brits call the leafy vegetable you Americans apparently know as arugula.

For us it's "rukola", which sounds like a compromise between the two :D

@Andrzej
Rucola evokes the memory of the old cough drop commercial, where an Alpine guy yodels a sort-of jingle for Riccola cough drops.

Andrzej,
Too soon. Much too soon.
Some stuff only makes sense when it comes too soon though.

@Andrzej

I'm much closer to this than you might think. I'm laughing (at my own joke, which is possibly a problem in itself).

@Andrzej

😀

So, the whole thing was to draw me into a dilemma, and then exploit which ever way I went.

Enjoy making fun of slightly daft old men, do you?  

And I don't believe your explanation of "rocket" for a second, mister.  

What's worse, I don't even really know what arugula is.  I think it was invented after I was a kid, because I don't remember any fights with my mother over whether I ate my "arugula" or not.

Thanks, you've ignited multiple generations of trauma with your little witticism.

IHYH.  (That's "I Hope You're Happy", which I'm hoping will catch on in internetese, and then I'll have a legacy.)

@Elena

Yeah, I think it's an odd saying since dancing is almost always on a bare floor.

@Elbridge Gerry

If it wasnt, then you'd cut the rug you're on!

@Bruce

Sorry, I meant that to read "music" references.

@Elena,

Lara who? 😄

@Bruce
Not "pain", challenges. Yeah, that's right, challenges. I'm sure that's what you meant. 😉

@Bruce Ah it makes me feel validated that that expression was several generations ago! 

Some pain and some gain as per usual!

commentBody:
@Big

How does it “miss the mark” and why are you “disappointed”? I can certainly think much worse words for scab that in my opinion are even more fitting.

@BigO
Is there a better word to describe someone who crosses a picket line?
@BigO surely that’s a word that applies to a behavior, and it’s pejorative because some people don’t like the behavior? Would “murderer” or “thief” also be unacceptable?

@Times Rita
Broke?

@Hanson

The most logical person in the universe, Mr. Spock, famously said, "The good of the many outweigh the good of the few.  Or the one."  (He was the one.)

Unions only work if everyone is in for the long haul.  Any gains won by the union will benefit everyone, even the scabs, to a much greater degree than being a scab would.

Most unions do not strike unless they have a contingency fund for strikers.

The fact that the union movement has slipped in recent years is a major contributing factor to the inequality of wealth issue these days.

@Jon
I've never crossed a picket line in my life and have walked a few. But I didn't post about SCAB for exactly this reason. The well tempered grid reflects a world -- a little skewed and peculiar but, like any world, with a spectrum of characters and events.

@Tim 

A 'beat' is any specific area, topic, etc., covered regularly by a particular journalist. Similarly, a beat cop is a police officer assigned to a specific neighborhood.
@Tim That's where I got stuck too

Tim and Peter, The U.K. noun definition here gives the same police officer as an example as the U.S. definition. We also use it here for the press; do you not in the U.K.? https://dictionary.cambridge.org/dictionary/english/beat

@Franco I haven’t yet done the Midi…. But I wanted to respond to  your comment here that the appearance of the word POLKA here put me in mind of my aunt Jeanette and uncle Matt, both of whom found each other later in life (in their fifties). My aunt, my mother’s sister, was a nun who left the convent in her 40s; Matt cared for his mother and sisters until his mother passed away. He was rather clumsy in social situations, but he was good to my aunt…and he seemed most alive when he was playing his accordion in a polka band he belonged to. This has nothing to do with the crossword, but I wanted to say thanks for bringing up the memory.

@Joe
Sentimentalist that I am, I love those family stories. The classic "Marty" with Ernest Borgnine remains a great example of the genre in fiction. And I think that Nancy Marchand starred in another one.
I hope Matt and Jeanette are still doing well and, if not  that they had a long and happy life together.
Thanks for the memories.

@Matt

World peace, climate, ____, Matt, ____, Tottenham

LOST CAUSES

🫳🏻
🎤

commentBody:
Matt,
No corner there either. 21st and Lex is a T intersection. Lex ends there, at Gramercy Park. Below the park, from 20th to 14th, it's Irving Place. More to the point, for tight theme you need more than a random NYC corner. Maybe 33rd and 3rd (a.k.a. toity toid and toid)?

Petrol,

And thus was born Artificial Intransigence.

@Petrol


You mad me laugh out loud ! Thank you

@Jane Wheelaghan
As a little kid, I remember my uncle driving his brand-new Edsel from Cleveland to NY to show it to us. His excitement quickly turned sour, and he got rid of the lemon after less than a year.

@Jane Wheelaghan
Speaking of intransigence, did the endless CATALOG throw you off?

@JJJJ 

I saw "Mini," and my first thought was "I bet it's the car." I did wait for a few crosses to confirm it, though.

Another X that hurts my ears is the one that people often pronounce at the end of ASTERISK.

@Strudel Dad

The Gaulic cartoon instead of the star?

@Strudel Dad

"Expresso" coffee.

@Bill
We axed that variant years ago.  But only after axing politely if we could do so.  (We’re Canadian, you know.)

@JohnWM

AAVE might encompass phrases like that...one does hear "AXE" instead of "ASK" .....but I've seen/heard it used to disparage folks (especially as Primary voting nears in this state)...so it made me wince.

Bill et al.,

Don’t get me wrong - “hurts my ears” does not mean I don’t occasionally say it myself. I love alternate pronunciations, and embrace their role in the vibrancy of language. I had heard before about the long-term use of aks for ask (c.f. fisk/fish).

I still sometimes get confused about and even intentionally binge/purge the two versions of nuclear/nucular.

And one I know only from my own weirdness (but maybe is more generally in play?) is calling a clafouti(s) - the French crustless tart - a ‘caflooti’. And actually, I have come to prefer caflooti :)

@JohnWM
May this patissier offer the suggestion to serve your calfouti with a side of whipped marscapone.

Bill,

Now you’re just messin’ wit me.
;)

I think it's more that Takis are new than that they're regional. They've only been sold in the US since 2001. Here in KC I'd say they dethroned the "hot chip" (flaming hot cheetos) around 2015 or so. I learned about them in 2018 when I worked with teens and couldn't keep enough Takis in stock to satisfy them. We also have a large Latine population, so that might be why they're so prevalent here, too!
